items.description: Public type `Greeter` with `greet(name: String) -> String` returning "Hello, <name>!" and `greet() -> String` returning "Hello, world!"

rules_checked.reasoning: Item is declared kind 'type' and describes a public struct/class named Greeter with two methods (greet(name:) and greet()). This is a concrete data/behavior type, not a protocol, service, or view — the kind label matches what is actually being built.

Target Sources/Greeter/Greeter.swift lives in the Greeter module, which is the sole target defined in Package.swift and already hosts GreeterModule.swift. Placing the public Greeter type in its own module's source root is the correct home; no cross-module misplacement.

Greeter.greet is a pure string-formatting function with no view, view-model, or data-access layering in this scaffold project — there is no higher/lower altitude to violate. The logic (returning a formatted greeting) is plain domain logic appropriately placed in a plain type, not smuggled into a view or view model.

The only existing type in the module is GreeterModule, an explicitly documented namespace marker/enum seed, not a suitable host for greeting behavior. Introducing a new Greeter type is warranted rather than extending an unrelated namespace enum; single responsibility (greeting formatting) justifies its own type.
The item introduces a concrete type only, no protocol/abstraction. No speculative protocol is being added, so this rule is satisfied trivially — nothing to flag.

Type name 'Greeter' is a noun describing its role; methods greet(name:) and greet() follow Swift API Design Guidelines (verb-based method names, argument label 'name' matches the parameter's role, and the no-argument overload omits an unnecessary label). Names carry appropriate semantics for their kind.

Greeter.swift as described requires no imports beyond Foundation/standard library for string formatting; it lives inside its own module target and introduces no dependency edges, so no forbidden import or reversed dependency direction is introduced.
